raw_consumer_statement:
On XX/XX/year>, I purchased a high-voltage electric scooter ( XXXX XXXX ) for {$840.00}. The merchant falsely advertised the product with an XXXX Waterproof Certification. ' After purchase, the merchant confessed to me in writing that the XXXX rating is fake and 'not yet certified. ' Furthermore, the unit suffers from a 67 % battery capacity failure ( delivering 18 miles vs. the advertised 55 miles ). 
I initiated a dispute ( XXXX XXXX ) with American Express XXXX XX/XX/year>, and provided chat logs proving this material misrepresentation. However, Amex recently closed the dispute in the merchant 's favor simply because I refused to ship the item back in standard consumer cardboard packaging. Because the battery has a 67 % defect, it is legally classified as a Damaged/Defective ( DDR ) lithium battery. Shipping a DDR battery in standard retail packaging is a severe fire hazard and a direct violation of DOT safety regulations ( XXXX XXXX XXXX ). 
American Express ruled that a store 's 'original box ' return policy overrides federal hazardous shipping laws and documented false advertising. I am holding the unit safely for a legal, HazMat-certified pickup, but I refuse to break the law to process this return. I am requesting the Amex Executive Resolution Team review my uploaded evidence and restore my permanent credit of {$840.00}.
